title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: According to the BRE manual, which of the following is described as the 'three-legged stool' of primary economic development customers?
options: Large corporations, small businesses, and government entities
Manufacturers, retailers, and service companies
Existing resident firms, local start-up enterprises, and out-of-market prospects
Local investors, regional developers, and foreign direct investors

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: What is the concept of 'customer equity' as described in Chapter 1 of the BRE manual?
options: The financial value of a company's customer base calculated for tax purposes
The idea that the more an EDO invests in a business relationship, the harder it becomes for that business to leave the community
A program that provides equity financing to small businesses in exchange for customer loyalty
A metric used to evaluate the return on investment of business outreach activities

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: A mid-sized manufacturing firm in your community is unaware of available workforce training grants and financing programs. Based on Chapter 1, why are small and mid-sized businesses particularly important to target compared to large corporations in a BRE program?
options: Small firms generate more tax revenue per employee than large corporations
Large firms already have embedded relationships with EDOs and financial resources to hire consultants, while smaller firms often lack awareness of available programs
State and federal regulations require EDOs to prioritize small business outreach over large employers
Small businesses are less likely to relocate, making them lower-risk investments for EDO resources

title: Business Retention & Expansion
chapter_tag: Creating and Managing a Business Retention and Expansion Program
question: According to Chapter 2, what is the first analytical step a community should take after committing to develop a BRE program and identifying team members?
options: Develop an annual workplan and budget
Launch a business survey to identify at-risk firms
Hire a program manager and establish a CRM system
Conduct a SWOT analysis of the community

title: Business Retention & Expansion
chapter_tag: Creating and Managing a Business Retention and Expansion Program
question: Chapter 2 states that 'no two BRE programs—even in contiguous markets—can or should be exactly the same.' What principle does this reflect?
options: Each EDO must use a different technology platform to avoid conflicts
Competing communities should differentiate their branding to attract different types of businesses
BRE programs must be tailored to each community's unique industry composition, economic resources, and organizational capacity
Federal funding requirements prohibit identical program designs across jurisdictions

title: Business Retention & Expansion
chapter_tag: Creating and Managing a Business Retention and Expansion Program
question: Your EDO is conducting a SWOT analysis and notes that Atlanta has a major strength in its transportation network. Chapter 2 cautions that this finding is insufficient. What level of specificity is required for a useful SWOT analysis?
options: The analysis must be macro and micro, specifying exact attributes such as the number of interstate highways, airport capacity, and rail connections that constitute the strength
Identifying the general sector (transportation) is adequate as long as it is benchmarked against national averages
The SWOT should focus only on weaknesses and threats, since strengths are self-evident to businesses
Broad categories are preferred to allow flexibility when presenting results to stakeholders

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: According to Chapter 3, how often should economic development outreach personnel conduct in-person facility visits with high-value targeted firms?
options: One to two times per year
Monthly
Once every two to three years
Quarterly

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: What does a Location Quotient (LQ) greater than 1 indicate when applied to a regional industry in a BRE context?
options: The region's industry employment is declining relative to national trends
The region has a higher concentration of that industry compared to the national average, suggesting a potential competitive advantage or cluster
The region is over-reliant on a single industry and is at risk of economic volatility
The industry qualifies for federal set-aside procurement contracts

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: An economic developer is preparing for in-person facility visits and is tempted to use a standard questionnaire for all companies. Chapter 3 explicitly warns against this. What approach does the manual recommend instead?
options: Use a standardized script to ensure consistent data collection across all firms visited
Keep conversations agile and organic, based on the unique characteristics and market realities of each specific business
Conduct all visits in groups to make firms more comfortable sharing information
Limit visits to large employers to maximize return on time investment

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: Chapter 4 characterizes BRE as a 'team sport.' What does this mean in the context of technical assistance?
options: EDOs must form internal teams of at least five staff members to manage BRE programs
No single organization can meet all business needs, so BRE requires active involvement of many distinct and diverse organizations providing technical assistance
BRE programs must be co-funded by both public and private sector organizations
Economic developers must compete with other EDOs to attract the best technical assistance providers

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: According to Chapter 4, what is the primary advantage of a cluster-based approach in BRE technical assistance?
options: It allows EDOs to reduce the number of businesses they serve by focusing on only the largest companies
It improves the competitive advantage of a region by addressing the concerns of businesses in a single cluster that share similar needs, maximizing available resources
It eliminates competition between firms in the same industry by encouraging collaboration mandated by the EDO
It qualifies participating firms for exclusive federal grants unavailable to non-cluster businesses

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: A regional EDO discovers that several manufacturing firms share concerns about supply chain disruptions, technology adoption, and talent acquisition. The EDO has limited staff and resources. Based on Chapter 4, which strategy would most efficiently address these shared needs?
options: Refer each firm individually to a separate set of state and federal agencies
Host a one-time trade show and consider the issue addressed
Focus resources only on the largest firm in the cluster, since its needs will represent those of all other firms
Implement a cluster-focused BRE approach: survey the cluster, convene a focus group to identify shared needs, and coordinate among all relevant EDOs and resource partners at a regional level

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: According to Chapter 5, what single factor is identified as the most important in business investment decision-making?
options: Access to capital and financing
Tax incentive packages
Proximity to transportation infrastructure
Labor

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: How did the Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act (WIOA) of 2014 reform its predecessor, the Workforce Investment Act (WIA)?
options: WIOA eliminated One-Stop Career Centers and replaced them with online-only service delivery
WIOA transferred full control of workforce funding from states to private industry councils
WIOA reduced the size of workforce investment boards, required unified state plans, and established data-driven accountability and performance measures
WIOA focused exclusively on disadvantaged populations and eliminated services for employed workers seeking career advancement

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: A community is experiencing rapid AI and automation adoption among its manufacturers. Traditional job-counting metrics used for incentive administration are producing misleading data. Based on Chapter 5, what adjustment does the manual suggest communities must make?
options: Eliminate all performance-based incentive programs to avoid inaccurate job counts
Require all businesses receiving incentives to use only traditional employment models
Reevaluate traditional methods of counting jobs and administering incentives to account for gig work, remote employment, and AI integration, remaining agile to the evolving nature of work
Lobby Congress to update federal job definitions before adjusting local programs

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: According to the IFRC definition cited in Chapter 6, what are disasters?
options: Only natural events such as hurricanes, floods, and earthquakes that cause physical property damage
Any event that results in more than $1 million in economic losses to a local business community
Government-declared emergencies that trigger federal disaster relief funding
Serious disruptions to the functioning of a community that exceed its capacity to cope using its own resources, caused by natural, man-made, or technological hazards

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: Chapter 6 highlights a disparity in disaster recovery outcomes across different populations. What does the research cited indicate about minority populations in disaster-affected communities?
options: FEMA studies show minority populations often have less access to remediation funds and services, face higher rates of mass displacement, and are more subject to managed retreat
Minority populations benefit from targeted federal programs that give them preferential access to recovery funds
Minority populations typically recover faster than affluent communities due to stronger informal social networks
There is no documented difference in recovery outcomes across demographic groups according to current FEMA data

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: Following a natural disaster, a coastal community's business owners are prevented from re-entering their properties for nearly two weeks. Inventory is destroyed and customers have left. Chapter 6 describes a strategy to prevent this. What does establishing a 'tiered system of business re-entry' involve?
options: Requiring all businesses to relocate temporarily to a designated recovery zone
Working with emergency management personnel to give critical businesses prioritized early access back into the community following a disaster
Establishing a lottery system so businesses can return on a fair, randomized schedule
Allowing only businesses with active disaster insurance policies to re-enter the affected area first

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: According to Chapter 7, how is venture capital defined?
options: A government-backed loan program for businesses that cannot obtain conventional financing
A revolving loan fund managed by community development corporations for established businesses
An equity investment in small, growth-oriented businesses, typically at the seed, start-up, or early expansion stages
A federal grant program administered through the SBA for minority-owned small businesses

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: What is the purpose of a 'claw back' provision in a business incentive agreement?
options: To protect the community by enabling recovery of incentives—through penalties, cancellation, or repayment—if a business fails to meet agreed-upon performance goals
To allow a business to reclaim overpaid taxes if it exceeds its job creation targets
To give EDOs the authority to seize business assets if a firm relocates before a defined period
To prevent competing jurisdictions from offering higher incentive packages to the same firm

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: A large employer has approached your EDO requesting a significant incentive package, claiming it will relocate unless incentives are provided. Chapter 7 describes a specific analytical test your community should apply before granting incentives. What is this test called, and what does it determine?
options: Cost-benefit analysis: whether the total economic impact of the firm exceeds the total incentive cost over a 10-year period
Return on investment analysis: whether the incentive generates at least a 3:1 ratio of new tax revenue to incentive value
The 'but-for' determination: whether the company would actually relocate or expand outside the jurisdiction but for the incentive in question
The market rate test: whether the incentive package is competitive with what neighboring jurisdictions are offering

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: According to Chapter 8, what is the National Science Foundation's definition of technology transfer?
options: The commercialization of federally funded research through private sector licensing agreements
The process by which universities patent and sell research innovations to manufacturing companies
A federal program that deploys DOD technology assets to support civilian economic development
The exchange or sharing of knowledge, skills, processes, or technologies across different organizations

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: Chapter 8 describes buyer-supplier programs as a tool for 'import substitution.' What does this mean in a BRE context?
options: Encouraging companies to purchase from local vendors, recapturing sales that were occurring outside the community and keeping dollars in the local market
Replacing foreign-made goods in local retail stores with domestically produced alternatives
Providing financing to help firms substitute imported raw materials with domestically sourced inputs
A tax strategy that allows businesses to deduct costs of switching from foreign to domestic suppliers

title: Business Retention & Expansion
question: A biotech startup in your region wants to license technology developed at a local federal laboratory but doesn't know how to structure the relationship. Based on Chapter 8, which federal mechanism is specifically designed to allow private companies to partner with federal agencies to share expertise, protect intellectual property, and accelerate commercialization of federally developed technology?
options: Small Business Innovation Research (SBIR) grant
SBA 7(a) loan program
HUBZone procurement set-aside
Cooperative Research and Development Agreement (CRADA)

title: Business Retention & Expansion
chapter_tag: Exporting as a Retention and Expansion Strategy
question: According to Chapter 9, what percentage of U.S. exports do small businesses account for?
options: 30 to 35 percent
10 to 15 percent
50 to 55 percent
65 to 70 percent

title: Business Retention & Expansion
chapter_tag: Exporting as a Retention and Expansion Strategy
question: Chapter 9 describes indirect exporting as a 'low-cost approach' to entering international markets. Which of the following is a key advantage of indirect exporting over direct exporting?
options: The exporting company retains full control over marketing, pricing, and customer relationships abroad
Intermediaries such as distributors and agents bring market insider knowledge, handle administrative tasks, and absorb local language and customs barriers on behalf of the exporter
Indirect exporting eliminates the need for any export documentation or customs compliance
It generates higher profit margins because the exporter avoids paying import duties in the destination country

title: Business Retention & Expansion
chapter_tag: Exporting as a Retention and Expansion Strategy
question: A local manufacturer is interested in exporting but has not yet assessed whether it is ready. Chapter 9 states that exporting is not appropriate for every enterprise. What does the manual recommend a company do before committing to an export strategy?
options: Immediately contact the U.S. Commercial Service and apply for an Ex-Im Bank loan to fund entry costs
Hire an international trade attorney to register trademarks in all target markets before beginning any planning
Partner with a competitor who already exports to share costs and reduce risk before independently entering foreign markets
Base the decision on careful analysis and sound planning, evaluating whether the company meets the organizational and financial criteria required for export readiness

title: Business Retention & Expansion
chapter_tag: Mergers and Acquisitions as a BRE Strategy
question: According to Chapter 10, what term describes a foreign company establishing entirely new facilities in the United States, as distinguished from acquiring an existing company?
options: Greenfield investment
Brownfield investment
Joint venture development
Strategic alliance formation

title: Business Retention & Expansion
chapter_tag: Mergers and Acquisitions as a BRE Strategy
question: Chapter 10 states that economic developers have a responsibility when their community is skeptical or resistant to a foreign acquisition. What is that responsibility?
options: To inform the community about how the new owner will benefit the local economy, since much public skepticism can be traced to misinformation
To block foreign acquisitions that are opposed by a majority of local voters
To negotiate a community benefit agreement with the acquiring firm before approving the transaction
To require the foreign owner to hire a fixed percentage of local workers as a condition of receiving EDO assistance

title: Business Retention & Expansion
chapter_tag: Mergers and Acquisitions as a BRE Strategy
question: A foreign company has just acquired a major employer in your community. Local business owners are concerned, and the EDO is unsure whether to treat this new foreign-owned entity as a local or foreign company. Based on Chapter 10, what is the correct approach?
options: Treat the facility as a foreign entity subject to additional reporting requirements and scrutiny since its parent is foreign-owned
Defer all engagement with the new owner until the parent company's global strategic intentions become clear
Recognize that U.S. operations owned by foreign companies do not always behave like their parent corporations and should be treated as distinct local entities with their own needs
Assign the firm to the business attraction team rather than the BRE team since it is now foreign-controlled

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Once established, entrepreneurs are ________; they tend to stay, grow, and create jobs where they were founded.
options: Messy
Fragile
Slimy
Sticky

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: An economic developer explains that entrepreneurship helps diversify industry sectors and stabilize local economies. What concept are they describing?
options: Tax abatements
Entrepreneurial ecosystems
Zoning reform
Corporate consolidation

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Which entrepreneurial stack segment focuses on commercialization of research and intellectual property?
options: Microenterprise
Technology transfer
Retail business
Franchise ownership

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: A founder starts a small online art shop generating supplemental household income. Which part of the entrepreneurial stack best describes this?
options: Startup/scaleup
Technology transfer
Microenterprise
Industrial manufacturer

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Why should economic development organizations avoid trying to provide every service themselves?
options: Federal law prevents it
Resource strain and lack of strategic focus
It limits collaboration opportunities
Entrepreneurs prefer isolation

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: An entrepreneur cannot obtain traditional bank loans due to limited collateral and credit history. Which ecosystem gap is most relevant?
options: Talent shortage
Capital spectrum gap
Overregulation
Infrastructure failure

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Technical assistance commonly includes which activity?
options: Union negotiations
Import tariffs
Corporate auditing
Business counseling

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: A new entrepreneur feels isolated and wants to meet others facing similar challenges. What support strategy should you recommend?
options: Counseling
Tax workshops
Peer networking
Debt restructuring

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Which nationwide program provides small business consulting and training?
options: HUD
SBDC
EPA
FEMA

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Your EDO surveys local businesses to determine gaps in technical assistance services. What is the main purpose of this action?
options: Meeting federal mandates
Identifying community needs
Reducing staffing
Marketing grants

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Mentor networks primarily provide entrepreneurs with what advantage?
options: Guaranteed loans
Real-world experience and guidance
Tax exemptions
Property ownership

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: A loan program requires participants to meet regularly to discuss business challenges together. What development strategy is being used?
options: Regulatory compliance
Peer learning within loan programs
Tax monitoring
Export promotion

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Economic gardening focuses on supporting which businesses?
options: Large multinational corporations
Local growth-stage companies
Government agencies
Import distributors

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Which of the following is NOT one of the 3 stages of growth?
options: Second Stage
High-impact
Start-up
Late Stage

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Which economic gardening effort is most useful to start-ups and second-stage companies?
options: Isolationism
Tax Abatement
Market Research
Advertising

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: An EDO collaborates with SBDCs to provide data-driven growth support to local firms. What approach are they using?
options: Economic gardening collaboration
Industrial recruitment
Tax policy reform
Export embargo

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Economic gardening differs from traditional recruitment strategies because it prioritizes:
options: External relocation incentives
Internal firm growth
Trade tariffs
Infrastructure expansion

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: A community invests in advanced market research tools to help second-stage companies expand nationally. Which philosophy guides this investment?
options: Economic gardening
Foreign direct investment
Tax abatement strategy
Industrial zoning

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Which section provides a high-level overview of a business plan?
options: Appendix
Executive summary
Operations audit
Supply chain report

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: An entrepreneur wants to know how to offer their product to consumers in the right place. What should you recommend?
options: Ask the Chamber about potential business locations
Advertise on TV
Build an executive summary
Conduct market research

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Which of the following is NOT one of the Four P's of Marketing?
options: Product
Price
Placement
Planting

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: A client struggles to determine staffing needs and workspace requirements while drafting a plan. Which stage are they in?
options: Closing the business
Formalizing the business plan
Export acceleration
Debt restructuring

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Most bank officers prefer a current ratio of _______

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: A new business is showing strong sales growth, yet fails to turn a profit. What is a likely explanation?
options: Fixed costs are depreciating
Lack of investment in variable costs
Variable costs are outpacing gross margin
External factors beyond their control

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: International business plans emphasize which activity?
options: Market research
Utility planning
Local zoning
Union bargaining

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: A small firm wants to expand overseas but lacks information about customers abroad. What should be the first step?
options: Secure patents
Hire legal staff
Conduct market research
Open a warehouse

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Benefits of direct exporting include all of the following except:
options: closer ties with overseas buyers
better exchange rates
more opportunities to learn how to increase competitiveness
greater control over the export process

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: A small business wants an easier, less time-consuming way to enter foreign markets. What do you recommend to them?
options: Direct exporting
Indirect exporting
Off-shore exporting
Safe-haven exporting

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: International planning requires addressing which additional complexity compared to domestic plans?
options: Time zone differences
Local advertising
Cross-border regulations
Internal HR policies

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: A company is trying to decide their approach to international sales. What should be one of their first steps?
options: Choose an export management company
Conduct market research
Choose an online platform
Hire additional staff to increase capacity

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
chapter_tag: Business Incubators, Accelerators, Makerspaces, and Coworking Spaces
question: According to Chapter 6, what is the primary distinction between a business incubator and a business accelerator?
options: Incubators are privately funded while accelerators are publicly funded
Incubators exclusively serve technology firms, while accelerators serve businesses in any industry

Incubators aim to help start-up businesses become sustainable, while accelerators focus on specialized strategy development and advanced technical training for businesses that already have a viable product or technology
Incubators provide physical office space only, while accelerators provide only financial capital

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
chapter_tag: Business Incubators, Accelerators, Makerspaces, and Coworking Spaces
question: According to InBIA's 2016 Impact Index cited in Chapter 6, which entity sponsors the largest percentage of business incubators in North America?
options: Private for-profit corporations
Local and state government agencies
Community development financial institutions
Academic institutions

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
chapter_tag: Business Incubators, Accelerators, Makerspaces, and Coworking Spaces
question: A community in a declining industrial district wants to launch a business incubator to stimulate economic activity among small, independently-owned firms. Based on Chapter 6, which type of incubator model best fits this context, and what services should it prioritize?
options: A technology accelerator with a venture capital network, prioritizing equity investment and IP protection
A virtual incubator with online-only programming, prioritizing digital service delivery over physical space
A community revitalization incubator, prioritizing business basics such as marketing, cash flow management, and accounting control for tenants in the declining area
A mixed-use incubator targeting high-growth software and mobile app start-ups to modernize the district's economic base

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: According to Chapter 7, what is a key characteristic that distinguishes angel investors from venture capital firms in terms of investment size?
options: Angel investors typically invest less than a venture capital firm but more than commercial banks, often filling the gap between $500,000 and $2,000,000 that is too small for VCs and too large for individual angels
Angel investors make larger investments than venture capital firms in all cases
Angel investors and venture capital firms invest identical amounts but differ only in whether they take board seats
Angel investors exclusively fund businesses in the seed stage, while VCs only fund companies post-revenue

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Chapter 7 notes that venture capital is geographically concentrated. According to the data cited, which three states accounted for 84% of total U.S. venture capital assets under management in 2019?
options: Texas, Florida, and Illinois
California, Massachusetts, and New York
Washington, Colorado, and Georgia
Pennsylvania, Ohio, and Michigan

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: An EDO is considering whether to directly provide financial support to local entrepreneurs. Chapter 7 identifies a key principle about entrepreneur contributions before any outside financing is extended. What is that principle, and what does it mean for the EDO's approach?
options: Entrepreneurs must demonstrate a college degree in business before receiving EDO financial assistance

Aspiring entrepreneurs must have 'skin in the game' by investing personal financial resources into the start-up before financial support organizations will invest, reflecting the level of risk associated with start-ups
The EDO must match every dollar of outside financing with a corresponding public subsidy to reduce risk
All entrepreneurial financing must be structured as equity rather than debt to align the EDO's interests with the business owner's

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: According to Chapter 8, what is the primary role of the Small Business Administration (SBA) in the lending process?
options: The SBA directly lends funds to small businesses through its own branch offices nationwide
The SBA guarantees a portion of loans made by third-party lenders to qualified small businesses that cannot obtain financing through normal lending channels
The SBA provides grants to small businesses that meet equity and diversity requirements
The SBA purchases equity stakes in small businesses in exchange for technical assistance services

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Chapter 8 describes the role of the economic development practitioner in small business capital access. Which of the following best captures that role?
options: Approving or denying loan applications on behalf of commercial lenders
Providing direct equity investment in exchange for a percentage of business profits
Administering federal SBA loan programs directly to businesses on behalf of the federal government

Increasing access to capital by connecting small business owners to an innovative financing ecosystem, identifying credit sources, recognizing financing gaps, and developing strategies to address those gaps

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: A small business owner meets with an EDO practitioner to explore financing options. She has already drawn on personal savings, credit cards, and a second mortgage, and has cut operating expenses. Based on Chapter 8, how should the practitioner interpret these actions?
options: As positive indicators that she has pulled in personal resources as expected — a prerequisite for accessing formal financing sources — and is now likely ready to explore external capital options
As warning signs of financial mismanagement that disqualify her from SBA financing
As evidence that she has exhausted all options and should be referred to a nonprofit charity rather than a lender
As irrelevant to the financing evaluation, since lenders focus only on business revenues and credit scores

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: According to Chapter 9, what percentage of U.S. businesses are owned by people of color, despite people of color representing 38% of the population?
options: Approximately 19 percent
Approximately 30 percent
Approximately 38 percent
Approximately 10 percent

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Chapter 9 discusses capital access disparities for entrepreneurs of color. What does the data cited reveal about Black and Hispanic founders' share of venture capital raised between 2015 and 2020?
options: They raised approximately 25 percent of total venture capital, reflecting their share of the general population
They raised capital at the same rate as white founders when controlling for firm size and industry
They raised over $15 billion combined, but this represented only 2.4 percent of total venture capital raised during that period
They received preferential VC access through federal set-aside programs accounting for 15 percent of VC deals

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: An EDO wants to better support entrepreneurs of color in its community who face systemic barriers to capital, networks, and technical assistance. Based on Chapter 9, which combination of strategies is most aligned with the manual's recommendations?
options: Focus exclusively on grant programs and avoid loans, since debt financing has historically harmed communities of color
Limit EDO involvement to awareness campaigns, since structural inequities can only be addressed through federal legislation
Partner exclusively with traditional commercial banks since they have the most capital to deploy to minority-owned businesses

Convene mentorship programs to expand networks, provide technical assistance such as legal aid and business coaching, and connect entrepreneurs with incubators and accelerators specifically designed to support entrepreneurs of color and underserved communities

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: According to Chapter 10, by how much faster do exporting firms grow in employment annually compared to their non-exporting counterparts?
options: 2 to 4 percent faster
10 to 15 percent faster
25 to 30 percent faster
No measurable difference was found

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Chapter 10 describes the 'reverse trade mission' as a common export promotion technique. What does a reverse trade mission involve?
options: Foreign buyers and distributors being identified and brought to tour a community's production facilities
Local companies traveling abroad to meet foreign buyers in their home markets
An EDO reimbursing companies for international trade fair attendance after the fact
A federal program that matches domestic companies with overseas distributors through an online database

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: A small manufacturing business in your community wants to begin exporting but lacks the resources to conduct foreign market research on its own. Based on Chapter 10, what is the most comprehensive role an EDO can play to help this firm succeed in exporting?
options: Refer the business to the nearest SBA office and take no further action, since export assistance is a federal responsibility

Help the business build export capacity by providing research assistance to improve competitiveness, develop export readiness through assessments and counseling, and identify the best markets and entry strategies through thorough foreign market research
Encourage the business to hire a full-time export manager before engaging with any EDO programs
Limit assistance to connecting the business with a foreign distributor, since market research is beyond the EDO's typical scope

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: According to Chapter 11, how are microenterprise loans typically described in terms of their underwriting approach?
options: Asset-based, relying primarily on the value of business equipment and inventory as collateral
Character-based, relying on the loan officer's assessment of the applicant's personal character and business potential rather than collateral or credit history
Credit-score-based, following the same criteria used by commercial banks
Revenue-based, using trailing 12-month sales figures as the primary underwriting criterion

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: Chapter 11 describes two main approaches to lending to microentrepreneurs. What is the key distinction between direct lending and peer lending?
options: Direct lending is administered by the federal government, while peer lending is managed by state agencies
Direct lending is for businesses with existing revenues, while peer lending is exclusively for start-up businesses with no track record
Direct lending offers higher loan amounts while peer lending is capped at $5,000 per participant

Direct lending is similar to bank lending where borrowers guarantee repayment through collateral or co-signers, while peer lending provides financing to groups where members are collectively responsible for repayment and rely on peer pressure rather than collateral

title: Entrepreneurial and Small Business Development Strategies
question: An EDO is designing a microenterprise lending program for a community with many low-income entrepreneurs who have no credit history, insufficient collateral, and limited business management experience. Based on Chapter 11, what type of lending approach is most appropriate, and what additional support must accompany the financing?
options: A standard commercial lending model with relaxed collateral requirements and no wraparound services, since over-programming can create dependency
A grant-only model, since offering any loans to individuals with poor credit histories is irresponsible and predatory

A peer lending or direct character-based microlending model, paired with robust technical assistance throughout the process — including pre-loan help such as business plan development and post-loan support such as cash flow management — since microentrepreneurs typically require a great deal of assistance throughout the lending process
Refer all applicants to the SBA Microloan Program without offering local wraparound services, since federal programs are better capitalized

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
chapter_tag: Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development (ELED) and the Ecosystem
question: According to Chapter 1, what is the core focus of Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development (ELED)?
options: Recruiting out-of-state corporations to establish headquarters in local communities
Concentrating resources on high-growth technology startups in urban centers

Growing economies by supporting business creation and accelerating the growth of existing businesses across all spectrums of industry, inclusive of business size, location, industry, and founder background
Replacing traditional economic development strategies such as business attraction with entrepreneur-focused programs

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
chapter_tag: Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development (ELED) and the Ecosystem
question: A newly hired economic developer wants to build an ELED strategy in her community. She finds dozens of existing entrepreneurship support organizations already operating locally. Based on Chapter 1, what should be her first priority?
options: Launch a new flagship incubator program to establish the EDO as the lead entrepreneurship organization
Conduct a needs assessment survey targeting only high-growth firms to identify where to concentrate resources
Seek state and federal grants to fund new programming before engaging with existing organizations

Discover what entrepreneurship supports are already in place, find the gaps, and knit together existing resources to form a strong support network rather than searching for a single 'silver bullet' program

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
question: Chapter 2 describes Louisiana Economic Development's approach to communicating program value to entrepreneurs. What key lesson does it highlight about messaging strategy?
options: Entrepreneurs respond best to data-heavy reports that quantify job creation and economic impact
Government branding should be prominently featured to establish credibility with business owners
Entrepreneurs don't necessarily care about job creation metrics; they are more motivated by peer testimonials showing how others resolved pain points similar to their own
Program structure and eligibility criteria should be the primary focus of all outreach communications

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
question: Louisiana Economic Development's SBS division expanded from a single program to a full suite of services without any budget increase. Based on Chapter 2, which combination of strategies enabled this growth?
options: Finding co-investing partners, running pilot programs for flexibility, increasing efficiencies over time, and shifting budget dollars strategically to seize new opportunities as they arise
Reducing program quality standards and accepting lower outcomes to stretch available dollars further
Consolidating all services under one department to eliminate partner coordination costs
Redirecting all funding from technical assistance programs into direct financial grants to entrepreneurs

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
question: Chapter 3 describes four organizational personalities in an entrepreneurial ecosystem. Which type is characterized as a 'CAVE person' (Citizens Against Virtually Everything) who uses 'no' frequently and blocks community energy and initiatives?
options: Historian
Sustainer
Revolutionary
Squelcher

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
question: Based on the Humboldt, Kansas case study in Chapter 3, what principle does the city administrator's role illustrate about government's position in entrepreneurial ecosystem development?
options: Government must fund and lead every initiative to ensure accountability and sustainability
Government can play a supportive, facilitative role — helping where it can and stepping back to let community members and private investors lead when they step up
Government should focus exclusively on recruiting outside businesses rather than supporting local entrepreneurs
Government participation is counterproductive in entrepreneurial ecosystems and should be limited to regulatory functions only

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
question: Chapter 4 quotes Andy Stoll of the Kauffman Foundation on why breaking down silos is essential in today's economic development environment. What is the primary reason he gives?
options: Siloed organizations tend to over-spend on administrative costs, reducing funds available for entrepreneurs
Federal funders now require inter-organizational collaboration as a condition of most economic development grants
With the increasing speed of the economy, no one individual can process all information and make decisions quickly enough — agility and diverse perspectives are required for smart decision-making
Siloed thinking is primarily a symptom of under-resourced organizations that need more staff and technology

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
question: An EDO in a mid-sized city is trying to build an ELED strategy but keeps encountering resistance from other organizations who fear being replaced or defunded. Based on Chapter 4, what approach does the manual recommend for building early ecosystem momentum despite this resistance?
options: Identify low-hanging fruit such as organizations with strong overlapping programs like mentoring, convene them, find ways to work together, bring in early wins, and tell those stories to build momentum — then move to the next launching point
Pursue a top-down mandate from elected officials to require organizations to collaborate
Eliminate competing organizations through consolidation before launching a unified ELED strategy
Focus all energy on a single transformational initiative rather than spreading resources across multiple small efforts

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
question: Chapter 5 applies Clayton Alderfer's concept of group boundaries to community ecosystem health. What does an 'overbounded' community look like in this framework?
options: A community with very rigid boundaries that becomes closed off to the environment, losing the capacity to adjust to external changes
A community with too many competing organizations pulling in different directions
A community that allows too much outside influence, losing its local identity and sense of direction
A community that has achieved optimal balance between internal cohesion and external openness

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
question: Chapter 5 describes the Puerto Rico case study with Colmena66 following a series of disasters. After collecting island-wide survey data from over 600 businesses, what three-stage framework did Colmena66 use to support entrepreneurs through the crisis?
options: Assess, Plan, Execute — focusing on business continuity planning, strategic pivoting, and long-term capital access
React, Refocus, Rebuild — mitigating immediate damage, helping businesses adapt quickly, and connecting entrepreneurs with tools and resources to emerge stronger
Stabilize, Restructure, Relaunch — providing emergency loans, negotiating lease deferrals, and connecting to federal disaster programs
Identify, Triage, Recover — cataloging all affected businesses, prioritizing those at greatest risk, and deploying technical assistance teams

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
question: According to Chapter 6, what does MIT research cited in the manual reveal about the mean age of a successful high-growth entrepreneur?
options: The mean age is 28, confirming entrepreneurship as primarily a young person's pursuit
The mean age is 35, suggesting early-career professionals are the most successful entrepreneurs
The mean age is 55, as experienced professionals have the most capital and networks to succeed
The mean age is 45, busting the myth that entrepreneurship is primarily a younger person's game

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
question: Chapter 6 identifies three domains of inclusion in ELED. Which of the following best describes the distinction between 'availability' and 'accessibility' of entrepreneurship support?
options: Availability refers to the number of ESOs in a region, while accessibility refers to the quality of services they provide
Availability applies to financing programs, while accessibility applies only to technical assistance and training
The two terms are interchangeable and both refer to whether support services are physically located in a community
Availability means a program or resource exists, while accessibility means that all entrepreneurs — regardless of background, geography, or circumstance — can actually reach and use it

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
question: Chapter 7 emphasizes empathy as an essential skill for ELED practitioners building inclusive ecosystems. How does the manual recommend practitioners develop deeper empathy for diverse entrepreneurs?
options: Have coffee meetings, hold focus groups, read research about women and entrepreneurs of color, attend their events and community meetings, and conduct direct interviews to understand their true challenges and opportunities
Complete formal academic coursework in cultural anthropology and sociology before engaging with underrepresented communities
Rely primarily on published demographic statistics and census data to understand the needs of diverse entrepreneurs
Partner with a diversity consultant to design all outreach programs on behalf of underrepresented communities

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
question: A practitioner is building an inclusive ELED strategy but finds that historically marginalized entrepreneurs are skeptical and disengaged from ecosystem activities. Chapter 7 identifies two primary relationship development objectives for this situation. What are they?
options: Developing strong relationships with groups historically left out of the ecosystem, and connecting those groups into the broader network of other organizations and individuals in the entrepreneurship ecosystem
Launching a marketing campaign targeting underrepresented groups and creating dedicated grant programs exclusively for minority-owned businesses
Hiring staff from underrepresented communities and requiring all ESOs to submit diversity reports annually
Creating separate parallel ecosystems for underrepresented entrepreneurs until sufficient trust is built to integrate them into the main ecosystem

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
question: Chapter 8 describes Daniel Isenberg's Scale Up Growth Barometer as a simple tool for measuring entrepreneurial ecosystem health. What two yes/no questions does it pose?
options: Is the local unemployment rate declining? Is the number of new business registrations increasing year over year?
Do entrepreneurs in your region have access to capital? Do they have access to quality technical assistance?
Is your region a good place for growing a business? Is your region getting better or worse as a place for growing a business?
Is venture capital investment growing in your region? Are startup failure rates declining compared to the national average?

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
question: An EDO director is pressured by funders to demonstrate impact using traditional metrics like venture capital raised and startup counts, but feels these don't capture the real progress being made in building an inclusive ecosystem. Based on Chapter 8, what does the manual recommend?
options: Abandon non-traditional metrics entirely and focus exclusively on what funders request to maintain funding relationships
Refuse to report traditional metrics and replace them with qualitative stories only
Commission an independent academic study every three years to validate ecosystem outcomes rather than relying on internal reporting

Recognize that the measurement framework must depend on what you hope to accomplish, look beyond traditional metrics to explore other ways of assessing ELED progress, and use stories and testimonials to bridge the gap between standard measures and the real impact of ecosystem work

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
question: According to Chapter 9, SourceLink's hierarchy of metrics aligns measurement to the stage of ecosystem development. What does the manual identify as the appropriate focus at the earliest stage of building entrepreneurial infrastructure?
options: Tracking venture capital deals closed and jobs created by portfolio companies

Inventorying existing entrepreneurship support resources and measuring how many are willing to participate in a collaborative effort, using metrics like number of network partners and entrepreneurial events
Measuring revenue growth and business survival rates of firms receiving support
Conducting a community-wide survey of entrepreneur satisfaction with available services

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
question: Chapter 9 notes that funding for economic development and entrepreneurship support is typically a local responsibility and is often unpredictable and not sustained. Based on the Brownsville, Texas case study, what innovative funding approach did the Brownsville Community Improvement Corp. use to build its entrepreneurial ecosystem?
options: Secured a single large federal EDA grant to fund all ecosystem activities for five years

Funded ESOs as part of its workforce development strategy, directly invested in entrepreneurs through an early-stage fund (Startup Texas), and leveraged private sector interest to grow the fund from $100,000 to nearly $500,000
Created a public-private real estate development fund and used proceeds to cross-subsidize entrepreneurship programs
Required all businesses receiving economic development incentives to contribute a percentage of their incentive value into a community entrepreneurship fund

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
chapter_tag: Business Retention and Expansion and Attraction
question: Chapter 10 opens with a quote defining BRE. According to that definition, what is business retention and expansion fundamentally?
options: A government program designed to prevent businesses from leaving a jurisdiction through financial incentives
A data collection process for tracking business closures and expansions in a regional economy
A private-sector customer retention strategy applied to economic and workforce development
A federal initiative managed by the SBA to support existing small businesses in underserved communities

title: Introduction to Entrepreneur-Led Economic Development
chapter_tag: Business Retention and Expansion and Attraction
question: The Scalerator NEO case study in Chapter 10 highlights several factors behind the program's success. An EDO partner initially feared the program would replace its existing services. How did the Morgan Foundation address this concern, and what broader lesson does this illustrate?
options: The Foundation provided financial compensation to competing organizations to secure their non-interference

The Foundation organized monthly calls for all ecosystem players before launch to help organizations understand their role, quell negative attitudes, and update the ecosystem on activities — illustrating that bringing partners along from the beginning is critical to building collaborative trust and reducing territorial resistance
The Foundation ignored the concern and let program results speak for themselves over time
The Foundation restructured the program so each partner organization received a formal co-leadership role, eliminating competition

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
question: According to Chapter 1, the Edward Lowe Foundation defines second-stage firms by what employee and revenue range?
options: 10 to 99 employees and $1 million to $50 million in revenue
1 to 9 employees and less than $500,000 in revenue
100 to 499 employees and $50 million to $250 million in revenue
50 to 249 employees and $5 million to $100 million in revenue

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
question: Chapter 1 shows a 2019 distribution of U.S. entrepreneurs by type: microenterprise (25.8M), main street (6.5M), innovation-led (966K), and second-stage (281K). An EDO is debating whether to focus all resources on innovation-led firms because they offer the highest growth potential. What does the manual caution against this approach?
options: Innovation-led firms are too difficult to support and require PhD-level expertise that most EDOs lack
Federal regulations prohibit EDOs from directing more than 20% of resources toward innovation-led firms

While innovation drives economic growth, disproportionate focus on innovation-led firms — which are a small percentage of the entrepreneurial community — risks excluding the vast majority of microenterprise and main street entrepreneurs who collectively employ millions and deserve support
Innovation-led firms generate insufficient tax revenue to justify the cost of specialized ecosystem support

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
question: Chapter 2 cites AEO research on the relationship between business ownership and wealth. What does the data show about the median net worth of Black business owners compared to Black non-business owners?
options: Black business owners have approximately the same median net worth as Black non-business owners
Black business owners have a median net worth 3 times higher than Black non-business owners
Black business owners have a median net worth 12 times higher than Black non-business owners
Black business owners have a median net worth 2 times higher than Black non-business owners

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
question: According to Chapter 2, how much of every $100 spent at a local business stays in the community, compared to what chain retailers reinvest?
options: $25 of every $100 stays locally — roughly equal to what chains reinvest
$50 of every $100 stays locally — about 50% more than what chains reinvest
$68 of every $100 stays locally — more than twice what chains reinvest
$90 of every $100 stays locally — nearly three times what chains reinvest

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
question: Chapter 3 cites a U.S. Chamber of Commerce Foundation study on innovation's contribution to economic growth. Approximately what percentage of U.S. annual GDP growth is attributed to increases in innovation?
options: Approximately 15 percent
Approximately 30 percent
Approximately 75 percent
Approximately 50 percent

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
question: A biotech startup in your community needs access to specialized lab equipment and cutting-edge research. Chapter 3 outlines several factors for successful corporate-higher education partnerships. Which combination of factors does the chapter identify as most critical for these partnerships to succeed?
options: Shared physical space, equal financial contributions, and annual performance reviews conducted by a neutral third party
Government co-funding mandates, standardized curricula, and mandatory internship quotas for all partner firms
Shared vision as a framework for activities, leaders who cross boundaries, a shared communication platform, clear intellectual property agreements, and long-term relationships
University ownership of all resulting patents, exclusive licensing agreements, and joint marketing campaigns

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
chapter_tag: Second-Stage Companies and Entrepreneurial Role Changes
question: Chapter 4 describes the Edward Lowe Foundation's four interior phases of second stage. In the 'Overwhelmed' phase, what does the manual identify as the critical milestone a second-stage leader must reach?
options: Recognizing they need external help and admitting they can no longer go it alone, though getting the right kind of help is easier said than done
Securing a Series A equity investment to fund the next growth phase
Hiring a professional CEO to replace the founder as the primary leader of the company
Achieving $1 million in annual revenue to qualify for second-stage support programs

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
chapter_tag: Second-Stage Companies and Entrepreneurial Role Changes
question: The EyeVerify/Zoloz case study in Chapter 4 illustrates how a university technology transfer led to a globally acquired company. What does this case study demonstrate about the role of the entrepreneurial support network in innovation-led company development?
options: University technology transfer is only viable when a serial entrepreneur with prior exits is available to license the technology
Corporate acquisition inevitably leads to the departure of a company from its home community, making ecosystem investment in such firms a poor use of resources
Federal grant funding is the primary driver of innovation-led company success, and communities without federal research funding cannot replicate this model

A well-connected local ecosystem — combining tech transfer offices, angel networks, accelerator programs, coworking space, and technical assistance — can take a university lab innovation all the way from patent licensing to global acquisition while retaining local presence and economic impact

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
question: According to Chapter 5, MIT research shows that a 50-year-old founder of a new company is how much more likely to be successful compared to a 30-year-old founder?
options: About 10 percent more likely
Nearly two times more likely
About 25 percent more likely
Three times more likely

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
question: Chapter 5 describes intersectionality as critical to effective ELED strategy. Using the example of Black women entrepreneurs, what problem does ignoring intersectionality create for an ELED program?
options: It leads to legal liability under federal equal opportunity statutes governing economic development programs

An ELED strategy that doesn't account for how overlapping characteristics like race and gender create distinct barriers — such as Black women leading in business creation but having the lowest average sales of any group — will likely produce outcomes far below expectations for those groups
It results in duplicate service delivery, with multiple organizations providing the same assistance to the same demographic groups
It reduces participation rates among white male entrepreneurs who feel excluded from programs focused on diverse groups

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
chapter_tag: Entrepreneurial Ecosystem Building in Rural Communities
question: According to Chapter 6, e2 Entrepreneurial Ecosystems proposes a different visual model for rural economic development than the traditional three-legged stool. What does e2 suggest entrepreneurship should be in rural communities?
options: One equal leg of the traditional three-legged stool alongside attraction and BRE
The foundation of economic development, upon which business expansion, retention, and attraction are built and enhanced
A supplemental strategy reserved only for communities too small to attract outside businesses
A replacement for traditional economic development strategies rather than a complement to them

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
chapter_tag: Entrepreneurial Ecosystem Building in Rural Communities
question: The Valley County, Nebraska case study in Chapter 6 demonstrates a long-term community transformation. What is the most significant evidence of systemic change cited in the chapter, beyond typical economic development metrics like job counts?
options: The City of Ord's property valuation rose 131% since 2000, school enrollment grew 14%, and 70% of high school seniors reported wanting to return to the community — reflecting a cultural transformation rooted in collaborative investment and a new sense of hope
The county attracted three Fortune 500 companies to open regional offices over a 20-year period
The county received a national award for rural economic development from the USDA Rural Development program
The county eliminated its economic development department after achieving full employment and no longer needing outside assistance

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
question: Chapter 7 describes a 'valley of death' in entrepreneur financing. What does the manual say is the EDO's role in addressing this challenge?
options: Directly providing equity investment to all startups that cannot access bank financing
Lobbying Congress to expand SBA loan programs to cover all capital needs of startups
Referring all entrepreneurs with capital needs to venture capital firms regardless of their stage of development
Assessing gaps in the funding continuum and rallying support to fill them, ensuring entrepreneurs have access to the right type of capital at the right stage of development

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
question: Chapter 7 presents data on capital access disparities for minority entrepreneurs. According to Rodney Sampson, while 60% of net new jobs come from venture-backed companies, what does the data reveal about the share of venture capital going to Black firms?
options: Black firms receive approximately 8.5% of all venture capital, proportionate to their share of the business-owning population
Black firms receive 15% of all VC investment due to targeted diversity programs launched by major venture funds after 2015
Black firms receive approximately 5% of venture capital, which reflects steady progress from near-zero levels in the 1990s
Less than 1% of annual venture capital goes to Black firms, with only about 200 Black and Latinx firms having raised over $1 million in VC over two decades

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
question: According to Chapter 8, by how much is the average tenure of companies on the S&P 500 projected to shrink by 2027, based on the Innosight study cited?
options: From 50 years in 1964 to 25 years by 2027
From 25 years in 1964 to 15 years by 2027
From 33 years in 1964 to just 12 years by 2027
From 40 years in 1964 to 20 years by 2027

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
question: The CASE program case study in Chapter 8 describes how to connect small businesses to anchor institution procurement. What does Natalie Rosado Ortiz identify as the single most important key to making anchor mission procurement work?
options: Mandating that anchor institutions spend a fixed percentage of procurement budgets with local small businesses through city ordinance
Creating a public online marketplace where small businesses can bid directly on anchor contracts without intermediary support
Requiring all small businesses to obtain national supplier diversity certifications before being introduced to anchor procurement officers

Having a dedicated internal advocate — ideally a full-time supply chain or supplier diversity employee — within the anchor institution who cares deeply about empowering local businesses as strategic sourcing partners, since internal champions are critical with so many competing institutional priorities

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
question: Chapter 9 quotes IEDC's Matthew Mullin on what it takes to change public policy for entrepreneurship. What two factors does he identify as most essential?
options: Media coverage and public petitions signed by a majority of community residents
Relationships and persistence — combined with a clear, specific goal and willingness to pursue multiple avenues even when faced with initial resistance
Large campaign contributions and direct lobbying by well-funded industry associations
Academic research publications and university partnerships that lend scientific credibility to policy proposals

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
question: Chapter 9 provides detailed guidance on influencing policy at different levels of government. When approaching a mayor about supporting immigrant entrepreneurs, what key boundary does Kauffman's Evan Absher say practitioners must respect?
options: Don't ask a mayor about immigration policy since they don't control it; instead focus on actions within their authority — like making the city more immigrant-friendly or adopting programs to help the immigrant community — and be specific about what you want them to do
Mayors should never be approached on immigration-related issues since these topics are too politically sensitive
All policy requests must first be vetted by the city attorney before being presented to elected officials
Mayors are only receptive to policy requests that are backed by unanimous endorsement from the local chamber of commerce

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
question: Chapter 10 recommends that ELED practitioners prepare at least three types of compelling stories to serve as community ambassadors. What are the three story types recommended?
options: A data-driven story, a historical story, and a future-vision story
A results-oriented story, an inclusive story, and a warm-and-fuzzy culture story
A job creation story, a business attraction story, and an innovation story
A founder origin story, a community challenge story, and a program impact story

title: Accelerating Growth Through Entrepreneurship-Led Economic Development
question: The Erie, Pennsylvania case study in Chapter 10 describes how the Innovation Collaborative changed its community's entrepreneurial culture. The community had been identified as extremely risk-averse with low entrepreneurial activity. What was the core of their strategy for driving cultural change?
options: Launching an effective PR and educational campaign — using awards, social media, and storytelling across multiple channels — to communicate what entrepreneurship is, why it matters, and how the community can support it, thereby shifting the culture toward greater risk-taking and startup activity
Recruiting high-profile tech companies to relocate to Erie to shift perceptions of the city as a viable entrepreneurship hub
Securing a major federal EDA grant to fund a five-year entrepreneurship acceleration program
Replacing the city's economic development leadership with entrepreneurs who could model risk-taking from within government
